content.text: Introduction to Brain Tumour Surgery in Dubai

Receiving a diagnosis of a brain tumour is an overwhelming experience that requires immediate attention from a highly skilled medical professional. As a leading neurosurgeon in Dubai and Tehran, Prof. Giv Sharifi understands the anxiety and uncertainty that patients and their families face during this challenging time. Brain tumour surgery in Dubai has evolved significantly, incorporating advanced technology and refined surgical techniques to improve patient safety and long-term outcomes. Whether you are dealing with a benign growth or a malignant condition like a glioma, the primary goal remains the same: to remove as much of the tumour as possible while preserving vital brain function and your overall quality of life. This comprehensive guide explores the surgical process, the various types of procedures available, and what you can expect during your recovery journey under expert care.
Understanding Brain Tumour Symptoms and Diagnosis

Recognizing the early signs of a brain tumour is crucial for timely intervention and better clinical results. Patients may experience a variety of symptoms depending on the location, size, and growth rate of the tumour within the brain. Common indicators often include persistent headaches that worsen in the morning, unexplained nausea or vomiting, vision changes, and sudden seizures. Some individuals might also notice subtle personality changes, difficulty with balance, or weakness in specific limbs. Because these symptoms can mimic other conditions, a thorough neurological evaluation is essential to determine the underlying cause and develop an effective treatment plan tailored to your specific needs.

Once symptoms are identified, your neurosurgeon will utilize advanced diagnostic imaging to map the brain and locate the abnormality precisely. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Computed Tomography (CT) scans are standard tools used to visualize the tumour structure and its relationship to critical brain pathways. In some cases, functional MRI or tractography may be employed to ensure that surgical planning minimizes the risk of damage to eloquent areas of the brain. Prof. Giv Sharifi emphasizes the importance of a precise diagnosis, as this information dictates the surgical approach and the necessity for adjuvant therapies such as radiation or chemotherapy following the initial procedure.
Types of Surgical Procedures for Brain Tumours

The surgical approach for brain cancer treatment is highly individualized based on the tumour type and its anatomical position. A craniotomy is the most common procedure, where a section of the skull is temporarily removed to provide the surgeon with direct access to the tumour. During this operation, advanced neuro-navigation systems act like a GPS for the brain, allowing the surgical team to navigate around healthy tissue with extreme precision. This technology is vital for complex cases, such as glioma surgery, where the boundary between the tumour and healthy brain tissue may be difficult to distinguish visually.

For tumours located in difficult-to-reach areas, minimally invasive techniques are often preferred to reduce trauma to the surrounding structures. Endoscopic surgery, for instance, involves using a thin, flexible tube with a camera through a small incision or natural opening like the nose. This approach can significantly shorten hospital stays and accelerate the healing process. Your surgeon will carefully assess your specific case to determine whether an open craniotomy or a minimally invasive method offers the best balance of safety and efficacy. Every decision is made with the goal of maximizing tumour resection while protecting your neurological integrity.
The Surgical Process and Patient Safety

Preparing for brain tumour surgery involves a multidisciplinary team dedicated to your comfort and safety. Before the operation, you will undergo comprehensive preoperative testing to ensure you are in the best possible condition for the procedure. Prof. Giv Sharifi and his team prioritize clear communication, ensuring that you understand every step of the process, from anesthesia to the immediate postoperative period. During the surgery, specialized monitoring equipment tracks your brain activity, heart rate, and oxygen levels continuously. This vigilance allows the surgical team to respond instantly to any physiological changes, maintaining the highest standards of care throughout the entire duration of the procedure.

After the tumour is successfully removed, the bone flap is carefully replaced and secured, and the scalp incision is closed. The tissue samples collected during the surgery are sent to a laboratory for detailed pathological analysis. This analysis is critical, as it confirms the exact nature of the tumour and helps your medical team decide on the next steps, which might include further oncology treatments. You will be closely monitored in the intensive care unit or a specialized neuro-recovery ward immediately following the operation to ensure that your neurological functions remain stable and that you are recovering comfortably from the anesthesia.
Recovery and Long-Term Care Strategies

The recovery period after brain surgery is a gradual process that requires patience and dedication to rehabilitation. In the initial days, the focus is on pain management, preventing infection, and monitoring for any neurological deficits. As you transition to the recovery phase, physical, occupational, and speech therapy may be recommended to help you regain strength and function. Prof. Giv Sharifi works closely with rehabilitation specialists to create a personalized plan that addresses your unique recovery needs, ensuring that you have the support required to return to your daily activities as safely and effectively as possible.

Long-term follow-up is a non-negotiable aspect of your journey, as it allows your surgeon to monitor your progress and detect any signs of tumour recurrence early. Regular follow-up MRI scans and clinical evaluations will be scheduled to keep track of your brain health. It is also important to maintain a healthy lifestyle and follow all postoperative instructions provided by your medical team. While the recovery journey can be challenging, many patients experience significant improvements in their symptoms and overall well-being following successful surgery. Your commitment to the rehabilitation process plays a vital role in achieving the best possible outcomes for your long-term health.

content.text: Frequently Asked Questions
How long does recovery take after brain tumour surgery?

Recovery time varies significantly depending on the size and location of the tumour, as well as the patient's overall health. Most patients spend a few days in the hospital for initial monitoring before transitioning to home recovery. Full recovery, including the return to work and normal activities, can take several weeks to months. During this time, your neurosurgeon will guide you through a structured recovery plan, which may include physical therapy to help you regain strength and coordination. It is important to remember that every patient's healing process is unique, and your surgeon will provide a timeline based on your specific clinical progress.
Is brain tumour surgery always necessary?

Not all brain tumours require immediate surgery, as the decision depends on the type, location, and growth rate of the tumour. Some benign tumours may be monitored over time using regular imaging if they are not causing symptoms or posing a risk to vital brain structures. However, if a tumour is causing neurological deficits, increasing intracranial pressure, or is suspected to be malignant, surgery is often the primary treatment option. Prof. Giv Sharifi will conduct a thorough assessment of your condition to determine if surgery is the most appropriate and beneficial course of action for your specific situation.
What are the risks associated with brain tumour surgery?

Like any major neurosurgical procedure, brain tumour surgery carries inherent risks, including infection, bleeding, or reactions to anesthesia. There is also a potential risk of damage to healthy brain tissue, which could affect neurological functions such as speech, movement, or vision. However, modern surgical techniques, including the use of advanced neuro-navigation and intraoperative monitoring, have significantly reduced these risks. Your neurosurgeon will discuss all potential complications with you in detail during your consultation, ensuring you are fully informed about the procedure and the steps taken to minimize these risks to the greatest extent possible.
Can I return to work after brain tumour surgery?

Returning to work is a common goal for many patients, and it is usually possible after a successful recovery period. The timing of your return depends on the nature of your job, the complexity of your surgery, and how quickly you recover your cognitive and physical functions. Your surgeon will provide guidance on when it is safe to resume professional duties, often suggesting a gradual return to work to avoid fatigue. It is essential to listen to your body and follow the advice of your medical team to ensure that you do not overexert yourself during the early stages of your recovery.
How does Prof. Giv Sharifi approach brain tumour treatment?

Prof. Giv Sharifi adopts a patient-centered approach that combines clinical expertise with the latest technological advancements in neurosurgery. By focusing on precision and safety, he aims to achieve the best possible surgical outcomes while preserving the patient's quality of life. His approach involves a comprehensive evaluation of each case, utilizing state-of-the-art imaging and monitoring tools to plan and execute surgeries with extreme accuracy. Whether practicing in Dubai or Tehran, he is dedicated to providing compassionate care, clear communication, and ongoing support throughout the entire treatment journey, ensuring that every patient feels confident and well-cared for during their recovery.
